intro:
The Seleucid Empire was one of the great Hellenistic kingdoms carved out of the conquests of Alexander the Great after his death in 323 BC. It took its name from Seleucus I Nicator, one of Alexander’s generals, whose dynasty ruled from Syria across Mesopotamia toward the borders of India. Its western capital was Antioch on the Orontes; its eastern seat was Seleucia on the Tigris. To the south lay its great rival, Ptolemaic Egypt, and between the two powers lay the land of Judea, which passed from Ptolemaic to Seleucid control around 200 BC.

Though the Seleucids are not named outright in the Hebrew Scriptures, they fill the visions of Daniel. The shaggy goat of Greece whose great horn was broken into four points to Alexander’s realm divided among his successors (Daniel 8:8; Daniel 8:22), and the long oracle of the kings of the North and of the South traces the wars between the Seleucids and the Ptolemies (Daniel 11:5–6). Out of the Seleucid line arises the “little horn” and the “contemptible person,” Antiochus IV Epiphanes, who magnified himself against the Prince of the host (Daniel 8:9–12; Daniel 11:21).

In his rage against the covenant, Antiochus IV plundered Jerusalem, outlawed the worship of the LORD, and in 167 BC set up “the abomination that makes desolate” — a pagan altar and unclean sacrifice upon the temple of God (Daniel 11:31; Daniel 12:11). His sacrilege kindled the Maccabean revolt under the priest Mattathias and his son Judas Maccabaeus, who drove out the oppressor and cleansed and rededicated the temple — the Feast of Dedication that Jesus himself later kept (John 10:22).

The Lord Jesus took up Daniel’s words about “the abomination of desolation” and turned them forward to the judgment soon to fall on Jerusalem (Matthew 24:15; Mark 13:14), so that the Seleucid horror became a pattern of every desolating power that exalts itself against the holy place of God. Yet the empire that defiled the sanctuary stands in the end only as a foil to the true and final temple, the body of Christ, which death itself could not hold and no abomination can defile (John 2:19–21).

link_tables.title: Places

cells: Antioch
Western capital on the Orontes; later cradle of the Gentile church (Acts 11:26)
Seleucia
Seleucid port founded by Seleucus I, gateway to Antioch (Acts 13:4)
Jerusalem
Temple city plundered and desecrated by Antiochus IV (Dan 11:31)
Damascus
Ancient Syrian city within the Seleucid realm (Dan 11:6)
Babylon
Mesopotamian heart of the eastern Seleucid domain (Dan 8:2)
Tarsus
Chief city of Cilicia in the empire’s northwest, Paul’s birthplace (Acts 22:3)

link_tables.note: Click any name to open its article. Markers for these places and the event below appear on the map above.

cells: The Abomination of Desolation
Antiochus IV profaned the temple altar, the desolation Daniel foretold (Dan 11:31)
Alexander the Great
Conqueror whose divided empire gave rise to the Seleucids (Dan 8:21)
Seleucus
General of Alexander, founder of the Seleucid dynasty
Antiochus IV Epiphanes
The ‘contemptible person’ who desecrated the temple (Dan 11:21)
Daniel
Prophet whose visions foretold the Greek kingdoms and the desolation (Dan 8:1)
Mattathias
Priest who began the revolt against Antiochus’s decrees
Judas Maccabaeus
Led the revolt and cleansed the rededicated temple
